atoms.atom_name: Concurrent BUILD forks do not lost-update maturity_map.yaml (map stays single source of truth)

notes.text: 2026-07-13 REGISTERED (A3_GO_AND_MAP_CONTENTION.md item 2, director-decided QUEUE, high rank). The real velocity cap on BUILD parallelism: N concurrent forks each read-edit-write the single maturity_map.yaml to record their level, and tree_lock serialises the git COMMIT but NOT the read-edit-write, so two forks lost-update each other's level bump. INTERIM MECHANISM already in force (THREE_LANES.md Lane 1): the orchestrator is the sole map writer -- forks touch only their code file_scope and report level back -- which removes the contention at the cost of a serial map-record step. This atom's BUILD makes that safe-by-construction rather than by-convention: options to design/choose between -- (a) each atom writes its own evidence/level to a per-atom status file (docs/design/atom_status/<id>.yaml) and the map is GENERATED from them (map stays the single readable source, no shared write); (b) an append-only journal of level transitions replayed into a view; (c) a lock-for-map-writes-only short critical section extending tree_lock. Requirement (director): map stays single source of truth, NO lost updates, PROVEN by a test that runs two disjoint BUILD atoms in parallel and asserts both level bumps survive. loop_stage idle: sequencing is a re-rank call, DISCOVER/FRAME workable now per epoch gating.

2026-07-13 FRAME DESIGN LANDED (Lane-3 discovery fork, doc-only, docs/design/WORKTREE_AND_MAP_CONTENTION_DESIGN.md). Mechanism CHOSEN with reasons: option (a) per-atom write-inbox files docs/design/atom_status/<id>.yaml -- each fork writes ONLY its own file (disjoint paths => lost-update impossible with NO lock), and a build-time tools/merge_atom_status.py folds each landed inbox's level_current/evidence/simplification into the hand-authored maturity_map.yaml, run once serially by the integrator. Map stays the single readable source of truth for the draw; forks NEVER open it for writing; the interim 'orchestrator-sole-writer' convention becomes structural, not conventional. Critical detail: do NOT regenerate the map wholesale (it carries hand-authored simplifications/provenance/expert_hour/depends_on) -- the inbox is a NARROW write-only field-set folded IN. Rejected option (c) lock-extension with a DECISIVE finding: tree_lock's LOCK_FILE resolves via __file__ to <this-worktree>/docs/observability/.tree.lock, so each worktree gets its OWN lock -- a tree_lock in a fork would NOT serialise across worktrees at all, and option (c) merely re-codifies the bottleneck anyway. Rejected option (b) journal (shared journal re-creates the concurrent-append race; sharding it just reinvents option a). Proof test spec written (tests/background/test_atom_status_merge.py: concurrent disjoint writes safe, no lost update after merge, map integrity/only-targets-changed, clean git merge, pre-flight conflict detection positive+negative). loop_stage stays idle (BUILD-gated); the H9 build IS the Half-B status-file layer of the WORKTREE design, done together with H10.

2026-07-13 BUILT L0->L2, target REACHED (general-purpose fork, commit bc32e130; orchestrator-verified: 6 merge proof-tests pass, epistemic PASS). The map-contention mechanism is real: tools/merge_atom_status.py folds per-atom write-inbox files (docs/design/atom_status/ID.yaml, disjoint paths so lost-update is impossible with NO lock) into the hand-authored map as a NARROW line-level text edit -- only the target atom block changes, every other atom byte-identical (proven: 61/61 preserved, changed-set exactly the two targets; same-atom double-target + unknown-atom abort before any write). This structurally replaces the interim orchestrator-sole-writer convention (THREE_LANES.md Lane 1); ADOPTION next: build-fork briefs write status inboxes instead of reporting levels, orchestrator runs merge. target 2 met, loop_stage idle.

atoms.atom_name: Synthetic future market generation beyond real 2016-2025 history

notes.text: 2026-07-10: generating NEW future scenarios/difficulty is CURRICULUM per CLAUDE.md R13 (the baseline/curriculum split) -- the director's own instrument, never built or content-decided unilaterally by the agent, even once the depends_on prerequisite is met. Any work here beyond the underlying mechanism/engine must be director-named and director-authored content, not agent-invented.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass (self-refill draw, no BUILD code -- depends_on: [W1_reveal_over_time] not yet resolved either, W1 itself idle pending M4). Confirmed zero prior art directly: sim/forward_curve.py::generate_forward_price() is a DIFFERENT thing -- a backward-looking decision-time pricing model over the real 2016-2025 history, not a generator of new ground-truth history beyond it. External research grounds this atom's own mechanism-vs-content boundary in two distinct real traditions: (1) NESO's Future Energy Scenarios -- a small number of NAMED, human-curated pathway narratives, now off its annual cycle (next full FES not due until 2028), confirming real-world scenario CONTENT is deliberately-paced and curated, not cheaply automated; (2) real energy-trading risk desks (KYOS/Montel/QuantRisk/academic 3-factor literature) generate many synthetic price paths from a single CALIBRATED stochastic MECHANISM -- mean-reversion, seasonality, jump-diffusion, regime-switching volatility (hidden Markov, MCMC-estimated), cross-commodity correlation -- narrative-agnostic, reusable across different targets. Maps cleanly onto this atom's existing mechanism/content split: the MECHANISM (calibrate a stochastic generator against the SIM's own real history, analogous to sim/forward_curve.py's existing seasonal calibration) is a real, scoped, buildable capability; the CONTENT (which specific stress target it's pointed at and named) stays the director's R13 instrument, matching FES's own real curated-pathway practice. Named next step for the eventual FRAME pass (not decided here): whether the mechanism reuses sim/forward_curve.py's existing seasonal_calibration.json as its starting parameters, or needs its own independent volatility-regime calibration (calculate_sigma_recent() today is a plain recent-window stdev, no jump/regime-switching term). Full findings: docs/market_research/synthetic_future_generation_w1_2.md.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass answers the named next-step question above (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Re-checked depends_on against the map's own dependency-met rule (loop_stage=='idle' counts as met, not just level_current>=level_target): W1_reveal_over_time is loop_stage=idle, so this atom's dependency is genuinely met, resolving the prior pass's own hedged phrasing ('not yet resolved either'). Read sim/forward_curve.py::generate_forward_price() and sim/risk_engine.py::calculate_sigma_recent() in full rather than summarising from memory: generate_forward_price() is ENTIRELY DETERMINISTIC -- EWMA spot estimate + a fixed seasonal-shape multiplier (from sim/data/seasonal_calibration.json) + a term premium scaling with sqrt(tenor) -- there is no random/noise term anywhere in it, it returns one point price, never a distribution or a path. calculate_sigma_recent() likewise only computes a backward-looking realised coefficient-of-variation (stdev/mean over a trailing window) -- a single descriptive statistic, not a simulatable stochastic process (no mean-reversion speed, no jump intensity, no regime-transition matrix estimated anywhere in this codebase). This sharpens the prior pass's mechanism/content split into a concrete build plan: seasonal_calibration.json's own real, already-calibrated seasonal shape IS legitimately reusable as a starting input (same seasonality a synthetic future should still exhibit), but the actual stochastic engine (the mean-reversion/jump-diffusion/regime-switching path generator itself) does not exist anywhere in sim/ today and must be built genuinely from scratch -- this atom is fully greenfield at the mechanism level, not merely 'different in purpose' from the existing pricing model as the prior pass put it. Next BUILD question (not decided here, epoch-gated): which single real calibration target to fit the new stochastic engine's volatility/mean-reversion parameters against -- the existing 2016-2025 Elexon SSP history itself (matching real historical statistical properties, the most defensible anchor) -- before any director-authored CURRICULUM content (the specific stress scenario to generate) is layered on top per R13.

atoms.atom_name: L1 national weather: coherent AUTOCORRELATED joint national temp/wind/solar signal (weather persists -- cold spells and still spells last; temporal autocorrelation is physics not noise)

notes.text: 2026-07-13 REGISTERED (WEATHER_PHYSICS_HIERARCHY.md, director P1, QUEUE -- DISCOVER/FRAME workable now, BUILD gated to Epoch 3 with W1_2_generate_futures). the CORE physics primitive. Architectural steer (director: the mechanism is ours): model a latent BLOCKING-HIGH weather regime (hidden-Markov / persistence state) that jointly drives cold + low-wind + high-pressure, so COLD-AND-STILL arises MECHANISTICALLY, not from a fitted Gaussian correlation that under-weights tail dependence. Cold-and-still is THE single most important GB power correlation -- draw temp and wind independently and the supplier-killing winter tail never occurs and every hedge looks fine (a lie). Solar anti-correlated with heating demand seasonally+diurnally; wind droughts persist days. DoD: temporal autocorrelation present; the joint cold-still tail demonstrated (SHOW the tail); winter-tail report compared to a real GB worst week (if our worst week is milder than reality, physics still wrong). loop_stage idle: FRAME design authored this session (docs/design/WEATHER_PHYSICS_HIERARCHY_DESIGN.md); BUILD opens in Epoch 3. atoms.atom_name: L2 regional weather: FEASIBLE, spatially-correlated regional deviations that are AGGREGATION-CONSISTENT with the national signal (demand-weighted regional aggregate reconciles to national -- an invariant, mutation-tested)

notes.text: 2026-07-13 REGISTERED (WEATHER_PHYSICS_HIERARCHY.md, director P1, QUEUE -- DISCOVER/FRAME workable now, BUILD gated to Epoch 3 with W1_2_generate_futures). L2, conditional on L1. AGGREGATION CONSISTENCY is the feasible-test and MUST be an invariant true BY CONSTRUCTION (project/rescale regional deviations so the demand-weighted aggregate == national), not by luck: a world where every region freezes while national is mild is impossible by construction. R15 mutation test: perturb one region off-manifold and assert the reconciliation invariant FIRES (residual > tol). Interim standing instruction: keep patching individual call sites only if a live bug actually surfaces -- no proactive build before the epoch sequencing names its turn. Level 0->1 (FRAME->BUILD): company/interfaces/bitemporal_event_log.py (BitemporalEventLog, real dual-time-axis valid_time/transaction_time semantics, 17 tests) + company/interfaces/point_in_time_view.py (PointInTimeView, constructed once per decision, wraps the existing MarketDataPort adapter for price/forward observables plus the new bitemporal log for settlement-restatement-sensitive facts, 12 tests) -- both scoped exactly to the closed gate's own Option A sizing ('1-2 files, a class + a handful of methods'). Near-term detector also built: .claude/hooks/block_point_in_time_read.py (HARNESS_BEST_PRACTICE_ADOPTION.md item 1a, now explicitly director-authorized, flags new company/saas code referencing a full historical dataset with no as_of/bisect bounding evidence). Consequence was real and repeated: the dial-weighted self-refill selector treats loop_stage=build as actionable, so it re-drew this exact atom on essentially every empty-queue supervisor grant this session (matching the same self-referential-fallback bug class already fixed twice this session in PRIORITIES.md backlog matching and the fingerprint-based stuck-escalation). Corrected to loop_stage=idle to match D2_three_clocks and this atom's own prose -- the field now honestly reflects 'blocked pending the advisor's epoch framing,' not 'ready to build.'

2026-07-11 EPOCH-2 FRAMING RATIFIED, loop_stage restored to build (docs/design/THE_VALUE_CYCLE_FRAMING.md, SPIKE slot 3 = 'M1 at full parallel depth'). Attempted the obvious next step (migrate estimate_price_volatility()'s call site in simulation/run_phase2b.py onto PointInTimeView) and found it would have been WRONG, caught before writing the code (R4 diagnosis discipline): PointInTimeView's market_data_port is backed by tools/market_adapters/frozen_2025.py -> tools/live_market.py, which reads sim/cache/elexon_ssp_full.json -- a frozen snapshot explicitly for the LIVE DECISION path (tools/run_live_decisions.py-style present-day company view; the module's own comment: 'Merged into the LIVE decision path only, here'). The historical BACKTEST simulation's own price records (elec_records/gas_records, what _price_history_as_of() actually bounds) come from a COMPLETELY DIFFERENT pipeline: sim/system_prices_history.py::get_system_prices_range() hits the LIVE Elexon API directly for the run's own date range. Migrating the hedge-decision call site onto PointInTimeView/MarketDataPort as originally planned would have silently swapped the historical backtest's real settlement-price basis for an unrelated frozen 2025-snapshot feed -- corrupting the entire historical simulation, not fixing anything. Also confirmed MarketDataPort has no bulk-history method at all (only single-point spot/forward/summary reads) -- would need extending regardless. REAL NEXT STEP (not yet built): either (a) extend PointInTimeView.get_history_as_known() to be backed by a BitemporalEventLog populated from the historical simulation's OWN elec_records/gas_records (not MarketDataPort), or (b) promote _price_history_as_of()'s already-correct per-call-site bound into a small dedicated as-of-view class wrapping the sim's own record lists directly, never touching MarketDataPort at all. Sizing/choice between (a)/(b) is the next real M1 depth-work session's first task.

2026-07-11 REAL CALL-SITE MIGRATION BUILT, level 1->2. Chose option (a) (one architecture, not two): `market_data_port` made OPTIONAL on PointInTimeView (same fail-loud-if-missing pattern already used for bitemporal_log), new `build_price_bitemporal_log(elec_records, gas_records)` populates a BitemporalEventLog from the historical sim's own records (daily-mean price per commodity/date -- fits the log's one-record-per-valid_time model exactly, since estimate_price_volatility() already aggregates to daily means internally, confirmed by reading its actual code before designing around it), new `PointInTimeView.get_price_history_as_of(commodity)` reconstructs the exact {settlementDate, systemSellPrice} shape the volatility function expects. Both real call sites in simulation/run_phase2b.py migrated (electricity + gas hedge decisions); the old `_price_history_as_of()` wrapper + its cache RETIRED entirely (not left as dead code) -- confirmed zero remaining call-code references (only historical comments). transaction_time==valid_time is a documented simplification (no real settlement-run revision modeling at the price level yet) with an explicit escape hatch: a genuine restatement would `.record()` with a later transaction_time, which `history_as_known_at()` already handles correctly by construction (untested for THIS use case specifically, since no real restatement data exists yet -- exit test's 'cannot see past now' half is now structurally true and tested; the 'restatement versions correctly' half rests on BitemporalEventLog's own existing test coverage, not freshly exercised here). 26 new/changed tests across company/interfaces/test_point_in_time_view.py + tests/simulation/test_run_phase2b.py, full related suites (company/interfaces + company/trading + simulation run_phase2b/hedged_settlement, 779 tests) pass, epistemic PASS (simulation/ importing company/interfaces/ matches the same already-accepted 'layering oddity, not a violation' precedent as W3_1_price_cap_binding). NOT yet done, level 3 still requires: the event-drained loop (sim loop currently steps, not events) and materiality gates + lazy valuation (the affordability mechanism) -- both untouched by this pass.

2026-07-11 DISCOVER+FRAME on the remaining two sub-components (docs/design/M1_EVENT_DRAIN_MATERIALITY_FRAME.md), BUILD deliberately NOT started. Investigated the real code before assuming the framing's abstract language meant a full rewrite: simulation/run_phase2b.py's OUTER decision loop (line ~780-902, all_terms interleaved chronologically then iterated once) is ALREADY event-shaped -- one iteration per real renewal/term event, not customer×tick nesting. The genuine step-based cost is ONE LEVEL DEEPER, in simulation/hedged_settlement.py's run_hedged_term/run_deemed_term/run_flex_term, each doing `for period in range(1,49)` per day -- but this generates REAL UK half-hourly settlement records, the actual ground truth every downstream number (billing, margin, this session's own CT/VaR/forecast-cashflow work) is built from, not wasted overhead a naive 'skip some periods' reading of lazy valuation would suggest. Real runtime today: 468-484s (~8min) for the full 2016-2025 run (docs/observability/sim-runner-log.md, 6 samples) -- not currently blocking anything, a genuine FUTURE scaling concern once M3 (population draws) and M4 (full replay) land, not a live problem. Zero prior art anywhere in the codebase for materiality/lazy-eval concepts -- genuinely greenfield. Deliberately did NOT attempt a same-day rewrite of hedged_settlement.py: it produces literal ground truth, a subtly-wrong change could silently corrupt years of historical data for a problem that isn't urgent (two-way-door filter: don't build against an unresolved design question when nothing forces the decision now). loop_stage set to idle -- genuinely blocked pending a director/advisor call on whether to pre-empt the scaling risk now or defer to when M3/M4 make it load-bearing, not a self-refill-answerable FRAME/BUILD task.

FORMAL EXIT TEST RUN same day: THE_VALUE_CYCLE_FRAMING.md's M1 exit test is 'the hedge decision literally cannot see past now; a restatement lands as an event and downstream values version correctly.' Half 1 (cannot see past now) was already covered for the real pathway (test_structurally_excludes_future_dates, company/interfaces/test_point_in_time_view.py). Half 2 (restatement versions correctly) had only ever been exercised generically at the BitemporalEventLog level, never through get_price_history_as_of() -- the actual pathway estimate_price_volatility() consumes -- an explicitly flagged gap in the prior entry above. New test_restatement_versions_correctly_through_price_history added closing that gap: records an initial price, then a later-transaction_time restatement for the same date with a different value; asserts a decision_time before the restatement's transaction_time sees the OLD value and a decision_time after sees the NEW value, both via get_price_history_as_of() itself. Result: PASS -- tests/company/interfaces/test_point_in_time_view.py (25 tests, was 24) + full related suites (company/interfaces + company/trading + simulation run_phase2b + simulation hedged_settlement, 780 tests total) all pass, epistemic verifier PASS (493 files scanned, no violations). Both exit-test halves now structurally true AND tested through the real hedge-decision pathway.

2026-07-12 EXPERT HOUR RUN, PASS (fresh-context phase-close-evaluator agent, no memory of the build, reviewing the L2 claim independently against the real diff/code/tests rather than the registered prose). Personally verified: (1) the dual-time-axis is real, not a stub; (2) both real call sites in simulation/run_phase2b.py are genuinely migrated and the old _price_history_as_of() wrapper is truly retired, not left as dead code; (3) both exit-test halves pass for real and assert what's claimed; (4) the M4 settlement-generation deferral is a real recorded director decision, not an unstated assertion. One low-severity adversarial finding recorded in expert_hour.findings above (same-day-price visibility boundary case) -- not a blocker, a genuine undocumented side effect for the next real touch of this path.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass quantifies the Expert Hour finding's real severity (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code -- level held at 2, not yet ready for L3). The prior finding called the same-day leak 'magnitude negligible... not a material future-data leak' without deriving a number. Traced the real consumer: company/trading/hedge_decision.py::estimate_price_volatility() (fed by the leaked history via decide_hedge_fraction(), the REAL per-term VaR-hedge-fraction decision) computes an EWMA (lambda=0.94) of squared daily log-returns, updated SEQUENTIALLY oldest-to-newest, so the final variance value's weight on any single return is (1-lambda)=6% for the MOST RECENT return and geometrically less for every older one -- the leaked same-day return (day D-1 -> D, using D's not-yet-knowable price) is structurally the SINGLE MOST HEAVILY WEIGHTED individual return in the whole 90-day EWMA, not an equal 1-in-90 contribution as 'negligible' implies. Also confirmed this is not a dormant code path: company/policy/decision_policy.py's CURRENT_POLICY (the real, live policy used in actual production runs, confirmed via tests/company/policy/test_decision_policy.py) has use_var_hedge_decision=True, so every VaR-hedged fixed/pass-through electricity term across the full 2016-2025 replay exercises this exact leaked path. Honest limit of this pass: an empirical measurement (the real GBP/MWh vol delta with vs without the leaked day, for actual historical dates) was NOT performed -- the raw daily elec_records are fetched live from Elexon per run and are not cached locally in a form this pass could reuse, and a fresh live fetch is out of scope for an autonomous DISCOVER turn. This pass therefore UPGRADES the finding from 'negligible, asserted' to 'analytically bounded at up to 6% direct EWMA weight on a real, live, per-term decision -- a real correction of the earlier reassurance, not just a repeat of it -- and still not yet a live-measured number, which remains the honest gap for whoever next touches this path (alongside the already-named fix: shift transaction_time to end-of-day D or start-of-day D+1).

2026-07-13 SYNTHETIC WORKED EXAMPLE closes the empirical-measurement gap named above using a controlled test rather than real historical data (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Real historical Elexon records remain unavailable locally (unchanged limit), but ran the ACTUAL production functions (company/trading/hedge_decision.py::estimate_price_volatility()/decide_hedge_fraction()) against a controlled synthetic 90-day price series, varying ONLY the final (leaked) day's price to isolate its effect. Result, real and code-executed, not estimated: a plausible cold-snap-magnitude spike on just the leaked day (1.2x/1.5x/2.0x/2.5x the prior day's price) moved the resulting annualised volatility estimate by +55.8%/+191.4%/+418.7%/+628.6% respectively -- confirming the earlier '~6% EWMA weight' analytical bound translates into a large real swing in the actual output metric once a genuinely plausible single-day price shock is injected, not merely a theoretical upper bound. HONEST ADDITIONAL NUANCE found via this same test, not asserted in advance: whether this vol swing reaches the final OBSERVABLE hedge_fraction depends on where the term's own computed value sits relative to company/risk/hedge_policy.py::COMPANY_MIN_HEDGE_FLOOR (0.85) -- in this test's own parameter regime the raw computed hedge_fraction stayed below the 0.85 floor even after the vol spike, so the floor clamped the visible output flat despite the underlying volatility estimate moving by over 600%. This means the same-day leak's real-world bite is regime-dependent: material when a term's own risk profile computes an hf comfortably between the floor and 1.0, invisible when the floor is already binding -- a genuine, precise characterisation, not a blanket 'always material' or 'always negligible' claim. Also corrects an error made earlier this session in a summary to Rich: this atom was misstated as 'level 3/3, hardened' in one prior turn's response -- the true, current state (confirmed here directly) is level_current=2, level_target=3, unchanged; that claim conflated this atom with H7_skills_and_rules (which genuinely did reach 3/3 this session). atoms.atom_name: DD attribution confound -- first entry of the 'selection-bias traps the company must discover' class

notes.text: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md director addendum item 9). NAMED ANALYTICAL TRAP -- registered here as the FIRST ENTRY of a named class: 'selection-bias traps the company must discover' (no separate trap-register infrastructure invented; the class lives as this atom + this note, per the staged doc's own 'note it clearly rather than invent infrastructure' instruction). The trap: the DD cohort looks like better payers PARTLY BY CONSTRUCTION -- failing payers drop OFF direct debit, so their bad debt lands in the cash/credit cohort. SIM truth contains the migration; the company's naive channel attribution (cost-to-serve and DD-discount-justification analytics built from observables) SHOULD BE CAPABLE OF BEING WRONG; the harness validates the company's attribution accuracy against ground truth (answer-key pattern, same shape as W2_7 willingness scoring). Live present-day relevance already logged: coldwalk:payment_channel_dd_fail_contradiction (adjudicated-real) -- payment_channel and dd_fail state computed by two independent pipelines that were never cross-referenced. Anchor: Ofgem payment-method debt research [L]. Build in M3.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Re-verified the 'live present-day relevance' citation directly against docs/observability/sanity_adjudication_ledger.json rather than trusting the reference -- coldwalk:payment_channel_dd_fail_contradiction is real, adjudicated-real, still open ('registered for a future consolidation pass, not fixed'): payment_channel (simulation.household_segments.payment_channel_for_customer(), a static per-customer profile) and dd_fail state (simulation.arrears_engine's payment_method()/payment_outcome(), a separate per-bill computation) are computed by two independently-dispatched pipelines never cross-referenced -- exactly this atom's own named trap, live in the actual codebase today, not hypothetical. External Ofgem-anchored evidence found, real and current (2026): 74% of UK consumers pay by direct debit, 13% standard credit, 13% prepayment; standard credit carries a HIGHER price cap specifically because it 'reflects the additional costs and risks in servicing' that payment-method cohort -- i.e. the REAL regulator's own price-cap methodology already treats payment-method cohorts as structurally different-risk populations by regulatory design, not an assumption this atom would be inventing. A real Ofgem statement found ('a generalised shift... to direct debit has reduced the numbers of customers who fell into arrears') is exactly the kind of surface-level correlation this atom's own trap warns against over-reading as pure causation without accounting for the migration-driven selection effect. No BUILD code, no level change.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass sharpens the prior pass's own finding with a more precise root cause (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Read simulation/household_segments.py::payment_channel_for_customer() in full rather than trusting the prior pass's 'two pipelines never cross-referenced' framing at face value: it is a PURE function of (customer_id, fuel) via a deterministic hash-seeded RNG, with its own docstring stating it is 'stable for the account's WHOLE TENURE' -- zero dependency on payment history, arrears, or dd_fail state anywhere in its signature or body. This means the real gap is NOT that two pipelines compute related state without cross-referencing each other (implying a wiring fix could reconcile them) -- it is that payment_channel currently has NO MECHANISM to change at all, ever, for any customer, regardless of payment outcome. The confound this atom exists to model (failing payers drop OFF direct debit, landing their bad debt in the cash/credit cohort) structurally REQUIRES payment_channel to be a time-varying state that responds to a real dd_fail event -- but today it is a fixed archetype assigned once and never revisited. This reframes the eventual BUILD's real first step: not 'cross-reference payment_channel and dd_fail' (a data-join), but 'make payment_channel a genuinely time-varying, event-responsive state' (a structural change to household_segments.py itself) -- a materially bigger and more precise first step than the prior entries' framing implied. Natural architectural fit (checked, not assumed): this project's own established pattern for time-varying per-customer state driven by discrete events is exactly simulation/household.py's Household dataclass + life_events.py's apply_events() (already reused this session for W2_4/W2_8's own findings) -- payment_channel migration is a fourth real candidate for that same substrate, alongside income_stress, rather than a new bespoke mechanism. No BUILD code, no level change.

atoms.atom_name: Per-run stochastic population draw (vs current fixed cast)

notes.text: director-confirmed known gap

2026-07-11 M3 DISCOVER+FRAME (docs/design/M3_DISCOVER_FRAME.md): population draw is genuinely greenfield -- CUSTOMERS is still a fixed list literal, _payment_rng is a single fixed seed (42+7919), zero draw_population/generate_population code found anywhere. Not currently a live epistemic violation (no seed exists yet for company code to read) but a real future risk if a draw module were placed carelessly (e.g. in saas/, which the portal already imports directly). BUILD deferred pending director/advisor sequencing.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER addendum (self-refill draw, no BUILD code): confirmed directly what the current fixed CUSTOMERS literal's real segment mix actually is (saas/customers.py) -- 15 resi / 4 SME / 5 I&C (24 total) -- the concrete marginal distribution any real per-run draw mechanism would need to preserve, not an abstract 'vary the population' instruction. External research grounds the mechanism choice in an established statistical tradition (population synthesis/microsimulation, not invented for this project): Iterative Proportional Fitting (IPF, established since 1940) is the standard technique for drawing a synthetic population matching known marginal distributions (e.g. this project's real segment/tariff proportions) while allowing genuine unit-level variation -- with known, real limitations directly relevant to sizing the eventual build: cloning-based IPF variants lose real heterogeneity (repeats existing customer shapes rather than generating genuinely new ones) and scale poorly as more attributes are matched jointly. A newer family (MCMC-based synthesis) is the alternative real practice where more attribute realism is needed. Named next FRAME question (not decided here): does this atom need IPF-grade marginal-matching rigour (defensible if segment/tariff proportions must exactly track the real book), or is a simpler weighted-bootstrap-with-replacement over the existing 24 real customer shapes sufficient for the stated goal ('vary the population run to run') -- a real sizing/scope call for the next FRAME pass, not decided unilaterally here.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass answers the named question above (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Read saas/customers.py directly rather than reasoning about IPF vs bootstrap in the abstract, and found a fact that reframes the whole question: CUSTOMERS is not a parametrically-generated table at all -- every one of the 24 entries is individually hand-authored with real narrative depth (named locations, specific tariff_type overrides for flex/pass-through mechanisms, specific write-off/vulnerability/life-event histories keyed to that exact customer_id). Confirmed via grep the real blast radius of touching this: 370 files under tests/company/saas/simulation reference specific customer_ids (C1, C3, C5, C_IC1, etc.) directly -- billing-tab tests, the year-spotlight dashboard's named-customer panels (phase-close checklist 0b requires a real named customer per phase), W2_5_life_event_stream's per-customer event stream, ledger write-off history, all keyed to these exact IDs surviving run to run. Neither IPF nor plain bootstrap-of-existing-rows was ever the right frame: IPF assumes an attribute-generation model to reweight, which doesn't exist here (there is no generator, only hand-written literals), and a full per-run REPLACEMENT population (new synthetic customer_ids each run) would break all 370 dependent references -- a real, wide blast radius, not a hypothetical one. FRAME decision: the eventual BUILD should be ADDITIVE, not REPLACIVE -- keep the existing 24 hand-authored customers as a fixed narrative core (preserving every downstream reference), and draw a per-run SYNTHETIC population on top of it (new customer_ids, e.g. G-prefixed or similar, generated from segment-proportion-matched attribute distributions derived from the real 24's own mix: 15/4/5 resi/SME/I&C) to deliver the atom's real_world_twin goal ('book composition varies run to run') without any one-way-door risk to already-built mechanisms. This also resolves the IPF-vs-bootstrap question as moot at this scale (N=24 core, small per-run addition) -- a straightforward weighted categorical draw over segment/tariff/region proportions is sufficient; IPF-grade joint-marginal matching would be over-engineering for a population this size. Next FRAME/BUILD question (not decided here, epoch-gated): what attribute distributions (consumption band, payment method, region) the synthetic add-on customers should draw from, and how many per run is realistic (a real supplier's book grows/shrinks by a small fraction per period, not a full reshuffle).

2026-07-13 FRAME pass answers the named next question with real anchors and a decisive internal finding (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Attribute distributions: docs/market_research/ASSUMPTIONS.md already anchors Direct Debit payment-method share (72% electricity / 75% gas, DESNZ Quarterly Energy Prices June 2026) and company/compliance/domain_invariants.py's TDCV_ELEC/GAS_LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH RangeInvariants already anchor consumption bands -- both real, current, already-coded, reusable as-is for the synthetic add-on customers' draw distributions. Region has no existing real population-level distribution in this codebase (an honest gap, not silently assumed). Sizing question ('how many per run'): checked saas/customers.py's own real acquisition_date field for every one of the 24 hand-authored customers directly rather than assuming an even cadence -- 20 of 24 were acquired in 2016 (the founding cohort), a further 4 trickle in across 2017-2020 (one per year, 2019 has two), and ZERO new customer acquisitions exist anywhere from 2021 through 2025 -- the book has been completely static for the entire second half of the ten-year replay. This is a real, decisive, quantified finding that reframes the sizing question from an abstract 'how many' into a concrete, scoped target: the synthetic per-run draw's most valuable and clearly-bounded first job is filling this exact gap (continuing new-customer acquisitions through 2021-2025 at a plausible rate matching the hand-authored cast's own established 2017-2020 trickle cadence), not an unscoped 'vary everything' goal. This also gives the eventual BUILD a natural, falsifiable success criterion: does the post-build book show acquisitions continuing through 2025, not stopping dead in 2020.

atoms.atom_name: Household budget sketch as hidden SIM state (income band, essential-cost floor, discretionary margin, savings buffer, priority-of-debts stack)

notes.text: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md item 1, director-decided). The core of the adjudicated finding: arrears are currently propensity-shaped; in reality they are the OUTPUT of a household budget meeting a price shock. This atom is that hidden SIM state -- income band (ONS-anchored income distributions/deciles), an essential-cost floor, discretionary margin, a savings buffer, and a debt stack with PRIORITY-OF-DEBTS ordering (rent/mortgage, council tax, food before energy -- anchored to debt-advice-sector priority-vs-non-priority-debt practice, e.g. StepChange/Citizens Advice guidance). Financial state joins the archetype reality the per-run population draw (W2_2) varies. WALL DISCIPLINE (record verbatim, AFFORDABILITY item 4): the SIM knows the budget; the company sees ONLY missed payments, broken plans, and what the customer discloses -- affordability is INFERRED through interfaces, never read. Anchors named, none encoded yet. Build in M3 with the population draw. LAW A/R12: never tune the budget distributions to hit a bad-debt number.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Income/margin anchored with real, current (2026) data: median UK household disposable income ~GBP3,225/month; critically, the aggregate 'GBP261/month left after essentials' figure masks real distributional structure -- the bottom 20% of earners face essential expenditure EXCEEDING income by GBP71/month, a genuinely NEGATIVE discretionary margin before any energy shock is applied, not merely a thin one. Essential-cost floor anchored directly and quantitatively: JRF/Trussell's Essentials Guarantee research gives GBP120/week (single adult) / GBP205/week (couple) for 2026/27; Universal Credit's April-2026 standard allowance (GBP98/GBP154) sits BELOW that floor by GBP22/GBP51 per week -- a real, policy-driven, structural shortfall for a material real UK population, independent of any energy price shock. IMPORTANT CORRECTION FOUND, not just confirmed (checked directly against Citizens Advice/StepChange rather than assumed): the atom's own registered debt-priority ordering ('rent/mortgage, council tax, food before energy') is NOT well supported -- real debt-advice-sector guidance classifies ENERGY AS a priority debt bracketed WITH rent/mortgage/council tax/HMRC (same tier, due to disconnection risk), not ranked below them; 'food' is not a debt-stack entry at all in this framework (no creditor relationship for future food purchases) and is better modelled as ongoing essential spending competing for the same discretionary pool. No precise within-priority-tier secondary ordering was found (a real, named open gap). Savings-buffer anchor not yet researched this pass (named gap, not skipped silently). No BUILD code, no level change. Full findings: docs/market_research/household_budget_w2_4.md.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER addendum, closes the savings-buffer gap named above (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Real, current FCA Financial Lives data, income-band-segmented: households earning under GBP15,000/year -- 22% have NO savings at all, over a third have less than GBP1,000. Households over GBP50,000/year -- only a small share lack savings, more than half hold over GBP10,000. Decisive finding: the savings buffer strongly CO-VARIES with income band -- should be modelled as a joint distribution, not two independently-drawn parameters. Population-wide baseline for sanity-checking: 1 in 10 UK adults have zero cash savings, a further 21% have less than GBP1,000. Additional real anchor: FCA estimates ~8.9 million UK adults in 'serious financial difficulty' (missed a bill payment in the last 6 months) -- connects this atom's hidden-budget-state concept directly to a real, sourceable population-scale calibration target. All five named components (income band, essential-cost floor, discretionary margin, savings buffer, priority-of-debts) are now real-anchored; only the within-priority-tier secondary ordering remains genuinely open. No BUILD code, no level change.

2026-07-13 SECOND SEARCH on the within-priority-tier ordering, still genuinely unresolved (honest, not abandoned). A second targeted search surfaced a real terminology tension worth registering, not resolving by picking whichever framing is convenient: some casual/summary sources use 'priority bills' loosely to mean 'food, energy, rent, council tax' together (essential must-pay COSTS), while the precise StepChange/Citizens Advice DEBT framework (already found) distinguishes formal priority DEBTS (a creditor relationship: rent, council tax, energy, HMRC) from ongoing SPENDING like food (no creditor relationship for a future purchase) -- both framings are real, from real sources, but answer different questions; this atom's own debt-stack concept should use the precise (debt-framework) sense, not the casual one, per the correction already registered above. No source found gives a strict tie-breaking order WITHIN the priority-debt tier (rent vs energy vs council tax when money is insufficient for all three) after two real, targeted search attempts -- confirmed genuinely un-anchorable via this method, not merely unsearched. Recommendation for the eventual FRAME pass: treat this as a real open question needing either a more specialised source (e.g. a debt-advice-sector practitioner survey, not a consumer-facing guide) or an explicit director-set curriculum assumption (R13), rather than a further DISCOVER search.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass, architecture (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). All four prior passes anchored external research; none checked internal code for the natural integration point, so this pass does. simulation/household.py's Household dataclass already carries a single field for this exact concept -- income_stress: IncomeStress (LOW/MODERATE/HIGH), 'updated by life events (job_loss, income_recovery, new_baby, retirement)' per its own comment -- and simulation/household_demand.py's HouseholdDemandRegister (already real, wired, per-customer, date-aware via household_at_date()/income_stress_at_date()) is the live consumer. Confirmed via direct read: NO income band, essential-cost floor, discretionary margin, savings buffer, or debt-priority stack exists anywhere in Household today -- income_stress is a coarse 3-level categorical proxy only, genuinely NOT the atom's own registered richer quantitative state. FRAME conclusion: this atom's real build should EXTEND Household (new fields alongside income_stress, not a new parallel register) and should treat income_stress as a correlated input, not an independent draw -- the same life events that move IncomeStress today (job_loss, retirement, and this session's own new illness/divorce additions from W2_5) are real, natural trigger points for the essential-cost-floor/discretionary-margin state to shift too, keeping the two atoms' shared substrate coherent rather than building two independent economic-state tracks that could silently diverge. This is the same 'one architecture, not two' principle already applied to several sibling atoms this session. file_scope for the eventual BUILD should include simulation/household.py and simulation/household_demand.py alongside the already-registered company/compliance/crisis_bad_debt_validator.py. Does not attempt the within-priority-tier ordering question again -- the prior pass's own recommendation (a specialised source or an explicit director-set R13 assumption) stands unrepeated.

atoms.atom_name: Life-event stream (job loss, illness, divorce, retirement, new child) -- ONE shared W2 event generator

notes.text: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md item 2, director-decided). ONE event generator serving BOTH the payment-difficulty triggers (this cluster) AND the adoption journey's key-moment conversion windows (C5_key_moment_conversion, registered 2026-07-11). Do NOT build two -- 'one substrate, two consumers', same principle as the reveal-over-time spine. Anchor event incidences to real UK population statistics (ONS life-events / labour-market flows / demographic rates) [L] where magnitudes are uncertain. Build in M3 with the population draw.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Real, current (2026) anchors found for each named event: unemployment 4.9% (1.76M people, Feb-Apr 2026) is a REAL, DISTINCT UK statistical category from economic inactivity due to long-term sickness (9.14M inactive, 21.0% rate, with its own ONS-tracked sickness breakdown) -- supports keeping job-loss and illness as genuinely separate hidden-state events, not merged. Divorce: 102,678 in England & Wales (2023, 'return to pre-pandemic levels'). New child: total fertility rate 1.41 children/woman (2024), the THIRD consecutive UK record-low year -- a real, ongoing decline, not a stable constant, flagged for the baseline/curriculum split (R13). Retirement: ONS confirmed to publish real State Pension age flow data, but the dataset surfaced in this pass only covers up to 2021 -- a genuine gap, not filled by assumption. atoms.atom_name: SME/I&C distress twin (sector shocks, insolvency events, late-payment culture)

notes.text: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md item 3, director-decided). The business-side twin of the household budget: sector shocks, insolvency events (bad debt PLUS a lost supply point, not just a write-off), and structural late-payment culture. Anchors: Companies House / Insolvency Service insolvency statistics; late-payment-culture surveys [L]. Note the segment T&C overlay differs materially for business (statutory late-payment interest applies -- see W2_9_segment_debt_tnc). Build in M3.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). All three named anchors verified with real, current (2026) data. Insolvency is genuinely sector-concentrated, not uniform: construction (17% of UK insolvencies), wholesale/retail (15%), accommodation/food (14%) dominate -- a real anchor for a sector-weighted hazard model over a flat rate. Insolvency procedure mix is 76% CVL (voluntary wind-down) vs court-forced routes -- plausibly correlates with a longer visible-distress run-up, a real detection opportunity echoing sibling atom W2_7's classify-under-uncertainty framing. Late-payment culture: ~GBP11bn/year UK economic cost, ~14,000 business closures/year linked to late payment (economy-wide, not energy-sector-specific -- flagged as an honest gap, not conflated). Statutory late-payment interest confirmed at 11.75% (base 3.75% + 8pts, mid-2026) -- directly anchors W2_9_segment_debt_tnc's T&C overlay. Live regulatory change found, not stable background: a Bill (introduced 19 May 2026) would cap B2B payment terms at 60 days and make statutory interest mandatory, in force no earlier than 2027 -- named as a live fact to revisit, not hardcoded as settled. 'Lost supply point, not just a write-off' mechanism confirmed with real UK practice (distinct from the different real mechanism of a SUPPLIER going bust/SoLR, deliberately not conflated): a vacated business tenancy's energy account liability transfers to the LANDLORD, continuing to accrue standing charges during vacancy -- the company's real loss is the customer relationship itself, not just unpaid balance; the meter transfers to a different, often less profitable counterparty rather than going dark. Named next FRAME question (not decided here): whether the hazard model needs a third hidden state ('vacant, landlord-liable') beyond a binary insolvent/trading flag. Full findings: docs/market_research/sme_distress_twin_w2_6.md.

2026-07-13 DISCOVER addendum, closes the write-off-rate gap named above (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Real, current (2026) figures found: total SME energy debt GBP1.3bn-1.8bn, up 9% year-on-year; unsecured debt per SME debtor up 13%, aged debt up 15% -- a real, current, worsening trend. Decisive write-off rate anchor: suppliers themselves estimate GBP1.1bn-1.7bn of historic SME debt -- UP TO A THIRD -- is never paid and is written off, directly confirming this atom's own 'bad debt PLUS a lost supply point' framing (energy suppliers are typically a low-priority creditor on insolvency, absorbing a large, not marginal, write-off share). Honest limit, not a gap in this pass's own effort: supplier-level/I&C-specific write-off rates are confirmed NOT publicly disclosed anywhere -- the ~33% aggregate is the best real anchor that exists, not an under-researched question. Remaining open item: the precise late-payment-to-insolvency overlap specifically for energy-sector SME accounts (the GBP11bn/14,000 figures are economy-wide, not energy-specific).

2026-07-13 REAL, ACTIVE DEFECT FOUND (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating -- registering the finding, not fixing it; the fix lives in simulation/life_events.py, W2_5_life_event_stream's own file_scope, not this atom's, and is BUILD code either way). Checked directly whether SME/I&C customers currently go through the SAME residential life-event generator as households, rather than assuming a segment gate exists: confirmed via live code execution (simulation/household.build_household_register() + simulation/life_events.generate_life_events()) that ALL of job_loss, new_baby, retirement_starts, illness, and divorce fire UNCONDITIONALLY for every customer regardless of segment -- read generate_life_events()'s full signature and body: zero reference to segment or property_type anywhere in the gating logic. Confirmed concretely against real I&C/SME customer records (C5/C6/C_IC1-4): these commercial-property Household objects carry a live income_stress field (initialised LOW, same as residential) and a live build_era field feeding directly into _RETIREMENT_PROB_BY_ERA -- meaning a commercial warehouse or office building can currently be assigned a 'new_baby' or 'retirement_starts' or 'divorce' event, and that event's income_stress transition then feeds simulation/arrears_engine.py's payment-default modelling for that SAME I&C/SME account. This is a real, ACTIVE absurdity-class defect, not a hypothetical future risk -- matching R10's own named C6 SME-as-Household-VAT precedent exactly (a business account modelled with residential-only real-world semantics). It directly explains why W2_6 (this atom) is needed as a SEPARATE mechanism, not an extension of the residential one: the current shared generator is not merely missing SME/I&C-specific distress dynamics, it is actively assigning the WRONG dynamics to that population today. Concrete next step for whoever builds this (not decided here, epoch-gated, and outside this atom's own file_scope): gate simulation/life_events.py's demographic events (job_loss/new_baby/retirement_starts/illness/divorce) to segment=='resi' only, and build W2_6's own sector-shock/insolvency generator as the SME/I&C replacement -- a class-level fix (R10), not a one-off exclusion. Flagged in docs/PRIORITIES.md for visibility given the live, active nature of this finding.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass, architecture for the replacement generator (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Follows directly from the active-defect finding above -- once the residential events are gated to segment=='resi', something must generate SME/I&C economic state instead; this pass frames what. Checked which of Household's fields are genuinely segment-agnostic vs residential-only: property_type (already has COMMERCIAL_OFFICE/COMMERCIAL_WAREHOUSE values), epc_rating, heating_system, insulation, solar/battery/EV all legitimately apply to a commercial premises' physical characteristics -- confirmed real commercial customer records (C5/C6) already populate these correctly. Only bedrooms (already None for commercial, correctly) and the DEMOGRAPHIC-flavoured drivers of income_stress are residential-specific. FRAME recommendation: do NOT build a wholly separate BusinessProperty/company dataclass -- keep the ONE shared Household dataclass (matching this atom's own name, 'distress TWIN', implying mirrored structure not an alien mechanism), and have simulation/life_events.py::generate_life_events() branch INTERNALLY on household segment: the existing 5 residential events for segment=='resi', a new sector-shock/insolvency-hazard event set (anchored to this atom's own real sector-concentration data: construction 17%/wholesale-retail 15%/accommodation-food 14% of UK insolvencies) for segment in ('SME','I&C'), both still writing into the SAME income_stress-shaped state variable if its existing 3-level granularity (LOW/MODERATE/HIGH) proves sufficient, or a segment-conditional richer enum only if the previously-named third hidden state ('vacant, landlord-liable') proves genuinely necessary -- start with the cheaper reuse, extend only if the FRAME/BUILD pass finds real evidence the 3-level model can't represent the business case. This is the same 'one architecture, not two' principle already applied to several sibling atoms this session, now resolving both this atom's own open architecture question and the active-defect fix's own 'what replaces it' question as one coherent design, not two separate problems.

atoms.atom_name: Can't-pay vs won't-pay -- hidden ABILITY x WILLINGNESS 2x2, classified by the company under uncertainty

notes.text: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md director addendum item 6). Hidden SIM state is a 2x2: ABILITY (the budget, W2_4-W2_6) x WILLINGNESS (attitudinal -- strategic/deliberate late- or non-payment DESPITE ability). Same missed payment, four truths; the company CANNOT observe the quadrant -- it must CLASSIFY under uncertainty from engagement signals, plan adherence, disclosure (the CLASSIFICATION capability is company-side, a C/F atom in effect; the hidden truth is generated here on the W2 side). Mis-classification physics both ways: can't-pay treated as won't-pay = customer harm + compliance breach (ability-to-pay rules exist for that quadrant); won't-pay treated as can't-pay = losses + moral hazard. Harness scores the company's classification accuracy vs hidden truth (answer-key pattern, same shape as the DD-attribution validation in W2_10). Anchor willingness incidence to Ofgem debt research / industry sources where possible -- tag [L] and VERIFY before encoding. Build in M3.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass, anchoring found (docs/market_research/willingness_classification_incidence.md), BUILD still NOT started (epoch 3, not yet current). Headline: the can't-pay/won't-pay framing is a live, contested UK political issue right now, not a settled classification question -- Ofgem's interim CEO was publicly criticised (Utility Week, 2026) for a 'reductive' can't-pay/won't-pay framing, and the End Fuel Poverty Coalition's own position is 'it's a can't-pay crisis, not a won't-pay one.' Implication for the eventual build: do NOT anchor willingness incidence to a naive/arbitrary split (e.g. 50/50) -- real advocacy and regulatory framing both point to can't-pay as the dominant quadrant; an inflated won't-pay incidence would misrepresent the real population and be politically loaded in the same way the real regulator is currently being challenged on. Quantified anchor found, WITH CAVEAT: StepChange H1 2024 data -- 41% of clients responsible for energy bills had arrears, 47% of that subgroup had a 'negative budget' (income below basic costs) -- real and sourced, but StepChange's client base is self-selected/already-distressed, NOT the general customer population this project's own draws model; this anchors ability-constrained incidence within a distressed sub-population, not the whole book, and must not be encoded as a whole-population parameter without that adjustment. Regulatory confirmation: SLC 27.8 requires suppliers to assess ability-to-pay and offer affordable repayment; Ofgem's 2025 Debt Relief Scheme is live, current, unresolved policy work -- reinforcing this is a genuine live uncertainty for the company to classify under, not a solved problem. Honest open gap (R10): no precise, quotable won't-pay incidence percentage was found this pass -- remains for the eventual BUILD/FRAME to either find via a more targeted search (e.g. StepChange's full 'Plugging the Gap' report) or explicitly acknowledge as un-anchorable, requiring a director-set curriculum assumption instead (R13).

2026-07-13 SECOND SEARCH, gap confirmed genuinely unresolved, two new real anchors found (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). A second, more targeted search for a precise can't-pay/won't-pay split still did not find one -- confirmed genuinely un-anchorable via this method after two real attempts, not merely unsearched. New real anchors found along the way: 55% of StepChange clients with dual-fuel bill responsibility were in arrears (March 2023, an updated/higher figure than the 41% H1-2024 figure already registered -- both real, from the same sub-population, at different points in time, worth keeping both since the real rate clearly moves); 5.3 million people live in households in debt to their energy supplier (Citizens Advice) -- a real, current, WHOLE-POPULATION-scale denominator anchor, distinct from the StepChange client-only figures. Recommendation for the eventual FRAME pass, not decided here: attempt a direct read of StepChange's full 'Plugging the Gap' report (not search-engine summaries) or adopt an explicit director-set curriculum assumption (R13), rather than a third search.

atoms.atom_name: Pay-but-don't-heat self-rationing (perfect payment record, consumption collapses below plausible living levels)

notes.text: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md director addendum item 7). SIM must GENERATE households whose payment record is PERFECT while consumption collapses below plausible living levels -- a vulnerability invisible in payment data, visible only in consumption analytics. Detection is a company-side C/F capability (self-disconnection / Consumer Duty class already in the regulatory oracle); a MISSED detection is a harm event. Anchor 'plausible living level' floors to the same Ofgem TDCV bands already in domain_invariants.py (do not re-derive). Build in M3.
