Confirmed the internal anchor already exists and needs no re-derivation: company/compliance/domain_invariants.py's TDCV_ELEC_LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH RangeInvariants (Ofgem TDCV 2026 review, e.g. Low band 1,400-1,800 kWh/year) are real, already-coded consumption floors this atom's own registration correctly points at. External incidence anchor found, real and current (2026): National Energy Action polling found 41% of prepayment-meter customers (~2M households) had gone without credit/energy access in the prior 3 months -- a real, sourced, quantified self-disconnection incidence rate for the PREPAYMENT sub-population specifically (not the whole customer base -- an honest denominator caveat, same class already flagged for W2_7's StepChange figure). Official 2026 fuel poverty rate (England, 2024/25 data): 9.4%, improving, driven by Warm Home Discount expansion + efficiency gains -- a real, current, DECLINING trend (baseline/curriculum split, R13, same as W2_5's fertility-rate finding). Contextual anchor for URGENCY, not this atom's own scope directly: UK suppliers owed GBP4.79bn in energy debt and the price cap rose 13% to GBP1,862/year, both as of July 2026 -- the real-world backdrop this atom's own vulnerability class exists within. No BUILD code, no level change. Full findings folded into this entry (no separate doc needed -- fewer, denser anchors than the household-budget/life-event passes).

2026-07-13 DISCOVER pass on this atom's own named company-side consumer (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Checked directly whether the 'detection is a company-side C/F capability' this atom's registration names for its own vulnerability class actually exists anywhere in the map or codebase: found real, decisive, precise evidence of the SAME orphaned-capability class already registered cross-cuttingly in PRIORITIES.md (2026-07-12). company/crm/vulnerability_register.py::VulnerabilityFlag.PPM_SELF_DISCONNECTED is a real, already-defined enum value (severity weight 5, mapped recommended actions ['offer_emergency_credit', 'debt_referral']) -- exactly the taxonomy slot this atom's own hidden-generation would need a company-side consumer for. Confirmed via grep: this flag is NEVER SET anywhere in company/simulation/saas code -- no caller computes 'perfect payment record + consumption collapse' and assigns it. The taxonomy exists; the detection algorithm does not; no atom in the 53-atom map currently owns building it (checked: no dedicated detection atom registered under this name anywhere). Real, precise finding for whoever authors that atom next (not decided/authored here, per EPOCH_GATING_AND_ATOM_AUTHORSHIP.md Rule 2 -- registering the finding, not unilaterally creating a new atom this pass): the target flag/action mapping already exists and is real, tested-shape infrastructure -- the missing piece is purely the DETECTION step (comparing a customer's real consumption history against the same TDCV floors this atom already anchors, filtered to accounts with a clean payment history), not a new taxonomy. No BUILD code, no level change.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass on this atom's own GENERATION side (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Checked the depends_on (W2_4_household_budget) dependency directly: genuinely met (loop_stage=idle), and this session's own W2_4 FRAME pass identified the real integration point (simulation/household.py's Household dataclass, currently only a coarse income_stress categorical). This pass traces WHERE income_stress is actually consumed today and finds a precise, decisive asymmetry: simulation/arrears_engine.py consumes income_stress_trajectory to drive the residential DIRECT-DEBIT DEFAULT pathway (a household under stress falls behind on payment, real, already wired, real DCA-placement logic downstream) -- confirmed via grep this is the ONLY behavioural consumer of income_stress anywhere in the codebase. There is ZERO consumption-suppression response to income_stress anywhere in simulation/demand_model.py, household_demand.py, or hh_consumption.py -- consumption today is driven purely by physical/occupancy factors (EPC, EV, solar), completely decoupled from the household's economic hardship state. This means self-rationing's defining behaviour (perfect payment record WHILE consumption collapses) is not merely unmodeled by omission -- it is the STRUCTURAL OPPOSITE of the only response path income_stress currently drives (default on payment, consumption untouched), not an adjacent gap. FRAME conclusion: this atom's real generation mechanism is a SECOND behavioural branch on the same hardship state W2_4/arrears_engine.py already models -- some stressed households route to arrears (already built), others route to self-rationing (this atom's own scope) -- rather than a wholly separate hidden-state mechanism. Reuses the existing income_stress/W2_4-budget substrate and the codebase's own established archetype pattern (simulation/household_segments.py's engagement/payment-channel/tenure archetypes) for the branch-selection probability, rather than inventing a new mechanism shape. Next BUILD question (not decided here, epoch-gated): what branches a household toward self-rationing vs arrears when both share the same underlying discretionary-margin deficit -- plausibly correlated with the same archetype traits (e.g. bill-shock aversion, payment-channel type) already anchored elsewhere in this codebase.

atoms.atom_name: Segment T&C / regulatory overlay on debt (statutory business late-payment interest, domestic late-charge exclusion, payment-conditioned tariff eligibility)

notes.text: 2026-07-12 REGISTERED, NOT BUILT (AFFORDABILITY_AS_SIM_PHYSICS.md director addendum item 8). Late-payment interest is STATUTORY for business (Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998 / LPCDCA, 8%+base) and effectively EXCLUDED for domestic; late-payment CHARGES are largely regulated out of domestic T&Cs. Tariff ELIGIBILITY conditioned on payment behaviour is real commercial policy (DD-discount tariffs; good-payer gating) -- model as company-side POLICY OBJECTS with fairness/oracle constraints, not as free-floating parameters. Anchors: LPCDCA (business); Ofgem SLC / Consumer Duty (domestic charge restrictions). Build in M3.

2026-07-12 DISCOVER addendum (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). Business-side statutory rate reconfirmed current: 11.75% (base 3.75% + 8pts, mid-2026) -- consistent with sibling atom W2_6's own finding, same real anchor, cross-checked not duplicated blind. Also found a live, current (2026) regulatory constraint directly relevant to this atom's own 'DD-discount tariffs / good-payer gating -- model as policy objects with fairness constraints' framing: Ofgem's Direct Debit Market Compliance Review is an ACTIVE enforcement effort (a real 2026 action sought improvements from 12 suppliers) requiring suppliers to set DD amounts off realistic consumption/tariff/seasonality estimates, avoid unnecessary large credit build-up, and actively return in-credit accounts toward a near-zero balance within 12 months. Implication for this atom's eventual build: a 'good-payer gating/DD-discount' policy object cannot be modelled as simply rewarding reliable payers with a discount in isolation -- real suppliers are simultaneously under an active regulatory obligation NOT to over-collect/over-hold credit from those same good payers, i.e. the fairness-constraint side of this atom's own 'policy objects with fairness/oracle constraints' phrase has a real, current, named regulatory anchor (this DD compliance review), not just an abstract fairness principle. No BUILD code; no level change.

2026-07-13 FRAME pass (self-refill draw, DISCOVER/FRAME only per epoch gating, no BUILD code). Checked whether real prior art already exists under a different name before assuming greenfield (the recurring mis-registration pattern found elsewhere this session) -- confirmed genuinely NOT: grepped company/billing/*.py (60+ modules) and company/compliance/*.py for statutory/8pts/11.75/late-payment-interest/segment-conditioned-rate logic, zero hits. The blank registration is honest; this is real greenfield, not another orphaned-module case. Found the right architecture to build it in, though: company/compliance/domain_invariants.py already has an EXACT working template for this atom's own shape -- vat_by_segment (VAT_RESIDENTIAL/VAT_SME as separate RateInvariant instances, a vat_rate_for_segment(segment) dispatcher, a check_vat(segment, actual_rate) checker, wired into a real consumer at company/billing/pre_bill_validation.py). This atom's own three named sub-scopes map onto the identical shape: (1) statutory business late-payment interest -- a RateInvariant per segment (business=11.75% current, resi=0/excluded) exactly mirroring VAT_RESIDENTIAL/VAT_SME; (2) domestic late-charge exclusion -- a boolean-style invariant (has direct precedent in this library's existing non-rate checks); (3) payment-conditioned tariff eligibility (DD-discount/good-payer gating) -- needs its own new shape (not a rate, a policy predicate), but should still live in this same module for the same reason W2_6's late-payment findings already anchor here (segment_debt_tnc and sme_distress_twin are named siblings). FRAME decision: extend domain_invariants.py (not a new standalone file) -- 'one architecture, not two,' matching the principle already applied to D2/G2/W3_2 this session. file_scope should be set to company/compliance/domain_invariants.py + company/billing/pre_bill_validation.py (the real wiring point, same as VAT) when this atom is BUILD-eligible. Next BUILD question (not decided here, epoch-gated): the exact statutory-rate effective_from date, since 11.75% = base(3.75%)+8pts and the Bank of England base rate is not fixed -- the invariant needs a real effective_from/rate history, not a single hardcoded current value, to stay honest across the simulation's own 2016-2025 span (rates were materially different in, e.g., 2020's near-zero-base-rate period).

atoms.atom_name: Ofgem price cap as a BINDING constraint on SVT pricing (not just a lookup)

notes.text: 2026-07-11 BUILT (SPIKE_WEEKEND item 1, MARGIN_REALISM Step 5): found the real gap by reading run_deemed_term() directly -- it computed spot*(1+deemed_premium) with ZERO reference to the Ofgem cap, completely unbound (the existing Phase 47a cap-clamp only ever applied to FIXED-tariff terms, never to deemed/SVT). Real UK deemed/SVT customers cannot legally be charged above the cap regardless of wholesale spot cost -- this is the actual mechanism that squeezed/killed real suppliers buying at spot through the 2021-22 crisis while capped on the sell side. Fixed: billed_rate = min(spot*(1+premium), get_cap_unit_rate_gbp_per_mwh(commodity, year)) for resi customers, 2019+ only (pre-2019 and non-resi stay uncapped, matching the cap's own real scope) -- same clamp convention already used for fixed-tariff terms, extended consistently rather than inventing a new pattern. New `cap_bound` field marks when clamping actually occurred. Margin can now legitimately go negative on a capped settlement period, matching real crisis economics (verified via a dedicated test). Note on architecture: this reuses the existing company/pricing/ofgem_price_cap.py import already present in simulation/run_phase2b.py (Phase 47a precedent) -- a SIM module importing a COMPANY module is a layering oddity (backwards from the SIM being the 'real world' company should discover, not vice versa) but is NOT a point-in-time-blindfold violation (the cap data is static, real, historical, not derived from anything a real company couldn't independently know) -- flagged, not fixed, consistent with the pre-existing precedent this change extends rather than a new decision. 6 new tests (post-2019 clamp, negative-margin-once-capped, pre-2019 uncapped, non-resi uncapped, gas-vs-electricity cap table selection, backward-compatible default). Real before/after effect on the margin trajectory (the 'bridge attribution' SPIKE_WEEKEND also asks for) awaits the next natural sim_runner cycle regenerating run_output_latest.json -- not fabricated from an isolated run under time pressure; to be written up once real post-fix data exists.

2026-07-11 HARDEN sweep Expert Hour run (SPIKE_WEEKEND item 5, energy-market-skeptic-veteran persona, read run_deemed_term() + ofgem_price_cap.py + all 6 cap tests directly): PASS overall (scope, year-boundary handling, commodity selection all correct) with one real gap logged, not silently accepted -- the cap table's ANNUAL granularity doesn't capture the real Ofgem cap's 2-4x/year updates, material specifically in the 2021-22 crisis window this atom targets (e.g. the real ~54% Apr 2022 jump). Recorded in company/compliance/sanity_adjudication.py ledger as expert_hour:W3_1_price_cap_binding (adjudicated-real). Not re-opening level_current (still 2/2, the binding-constraint mechanism itself is correct) -- this is granularity debt on an already-working mechanism, tracked for a future intra-year cap-table pass if crisis-period precision becomes load-bearing.

atoms.atom_name: Real settlement timetable (D+1/D+... true-up) as an explicit world mechanism

notes.text: 2026-07-12 DISCOVER pass (self-refill draw, no BUILD code). This atom's registration was entirely blank; checked internal code directly before assuming zero prior art. Found REAL, decisive, ADJACENT prior art that does NOT satisfy this atom's own scope: company/regulatory/settlement_reconciliation.py is a real, Elexon-anchored (Settlement Performance Reports) COMPANY-SIDE exposure/risk model of the real UK R1/R2/R3/RF settlement-run timetable (1/3/5/28 months post-delivery, 60/25/12/3% of adjustment volume resolved at each run respectively, +-0.5%/+-4% variance bands for HH/non-HH) -- genuinely wired (saas/reporting/annual_report.py real call site). But this is the COMPANY estimating its OWN future exposure/risk from a timetable it does not directly observe -- confirmed via grep that ZERO sim/ or simulation/ code implements the actual WORLD-SIDE mechanism (no R1_MONTHS/RF_MONTHS/settlement_run concept anywhere in sim/simulation/) -- the SIM itself still computes settlement as a single, final-form pass with no multi-run revision timetable at all. This atom's own scope (the WORLD mechanism itself, company/regulatory/settlement_reconciliation.py's real subject matter) is genuinely greenfield, not mis-registered -- the blank registration was accurate. Named concrete next FRAME question (not decided here): the natural architecture for building this is NOT a new one-off mechanism -- W1_reveal_over_time's existing BitemporalEventLog/PointInTimeView spine (valid_time=settlement day, transaction_time=each real R1/R2/R3/RF run's own publication date) already models exactly this shape (a fact revised at known-real, later, discrete points in time) and could host settlement-timetable revisions without inventing a second architecture, matching the 'one architecture, not two' principle already applied to D2/G2. company/regulatory/settlement_reconciliation.py's own real month-offset/share constants are a ready-made, already-Elexon-sourced calibration input for whichever future build picks this up -- do not re-derive them independently.

2026-07-13 BUILD OPENED (loop_stage idle->build) by DIRECTOR_TWIN approval -- the director delegated BUILD-within-the-open-epoch to the twin in-console (DIRECTOR_CANON.md v2 section 3a); ask_twin returned APPROVED, confidence high, for all six ranked Epoch-2 candidates (director_twin_log.jsonl entry 2026-07-13T07:33:20). Now BUILD-eligible for the supervisor's BUILD-tier draw. W1 built first; the rest concurrent where file_scopes are disjoint.

2026-07-13 BUILT (this pass), level 0->1, honestly NOT 2: simulation/settlement_timetable.py::emit_settlement_timetable(), additive-only, following the FRAME decision exactly -- built on company/interfaces/bitemporal_event_log.py::BitemporalEventLog (not a new mechanism), valid_time=the settlement day the figure is ABOUT, transaction_time=each real run's own publication date (delivery_date + relativedelta(months=1/3/5/28) for R1/R2/R3/RF), value=the progressively-revised figure (cumulative share of the gap: 60/85/97/100%, RF resolving EXACTLY to the true final value since the four shares are asserted to sum to 1.0). A plausibility check (not output tuning, R12) validates the caller's requested gap against the real +-0.5%(HH)/+-4%(non-HH) variance bands, raising unless allow_out_of_band=True for a deliberate stress case. WALL DECISIONS made explicitly, not glossed (per .claude/rules/epistemic-wall-sim.md's own instruction to flag rather than assume): (1) DID import BitemporalEventLog/BitemporalRecord from company/interfaces/bitemporal_event_log.py -- judged not a violation because that module is explicitly self-documented as the shared seam class (company/interfaces/ is the one path exempt from tools/epistemic_verifier.py's own check), is stateless/dependency-free (no shared global instance, no company business data), and this module constructs its own private instance -- no company state read, no sim state leaked either direction. (2) Did NOT import company/regulatory/settlement_reconciliation.py's own R1_MONTHS/share/variance constants, since that module IS ordinary company-side business logic (not the seam) and .claude/rules/epistemic-wall-sim.md is unambiguous that sim-side code must not import company.*/saas.* outside company/interfaces/sim_interface.py -- duplicated the constants instead, with an explicit WALL NOTE citing settlement_reconciliation.py as the single source of truth, and tests/simulation/test_settlement_timetable.py::TestConstantsMatchSharedSource imports settlement_reconciliation.py directly (tests/ are EXEMPT_PATHS, may import anything) to assert the two constant sets never silently drift apart -- a real, mechanical drift-guard, not just a comment. tools/epistemic_verifier.py run both diff-mode (--files) and full (496 files) -- PASS, zero violations. 17 new tests (11 in TestEmitSettlementTimetable/TestConstantsMatchSharedSource + 6 in TestRevealOverTimeProperty) + 109 existing company/interfaces/ tests, 126 total, all passing -- SIM_FAST_MODE=1 python3 -m pytest tests/simulation/test_settlement_timetable.py tests/company/interfaces/ -q. Proves exactly the property this atom names: a query as-of a date before R1 sees only the initial (delivery-time) figure, as-of after R2 sees the R2-revised figure (and NOT the final value), and RF resolves EXACTLY to the true value; also proves the general reveal-over-time property (as-of each run's own real publication date returns that run's own revision, no earlier and no later). HONEST GAP, why level_current stays 1 not 2 (target): this is the standalone mechanism, correct and tested against the existing bitemporal spine -- but it is NOT yet wired into a real settlement pipeline. simulation/hedged_settlement.py and simulation/settlement.py (the SIM's actual ground-truth settlement generators) still compute a single final-form value with no call into emit_settlement_timetable() anywhere -- deliberately not touched this pass, per this task's own additive-only instruction (do not modify existing settlement/ground-truth generation). L2 (this atom's own real target) concretely needs: (a) a real call site in one of those generators (or a new orchestration script matching simulation/README.md's pattern) that produces an actual settlement series' initial value at delivery time and its true final value from real settlement data, feeding both into emit_settlement_timetable() to produce the real revision sequence, and (b) something downstream (a report, a company-side reader via the sim_interface seam, or a test asserting an end-to-end pipeline read) that actually consumes the revised series rather than the mechanism only being exercised by its own unit tests. loop_stage stays build (not harden/idle): that integration is the concrete, named, real next increment, not open-ended.

2026-07-13 BUILT L1->L2, target REACHED (sim-engineer fork, commit 314f7721 pushed; orchestrator-verified HARD per R1 as this touches ground-truth: 10/10 new final-neutrality+blindfold tests pass, 81/81 settlement-family regression pass -- proof existing generators unchanged -- epistemic PASS 498 files, and the commit is PURELY ADDITIVE = 2 new files, ZERO existing files modified, the strongest possible final-value-neutrality proof; the auto-process run confirms net margin still £1,505,250, unchanged). New simulation/settlement_run_series.py::build_settlement_revision_log() is the real L2 call site: it wires simulation/settlement.py::run_settlement (the real settled book) + simulation/meter_reads.py::generate_meter_read_log (the real delivery-time estimate) into emit_settlement_timetable() to produce a real, non-degenerate revision sequence. FINAL-VALUE-NEUTRALITY BY CONSTRUCTION (the core risk control, held): true_final = sum of the real settled records (READ, never mutated) and is fed as RF's true_final_value which the RF run resolves to EXACTLY; initial = true_final x (estimated_consumption / true_consumption) where estimated is the meter-read model's own estimate built ONLY from a customer's prior confirmed reads (never this period's true value); the gap is thus a GENUINE estimated-vs-actual difference from an existing mechanism, not a tuned/invented number (R12/R13 honoured -- fidelity blind to P&L). No settled record mutated (test_settled_records_are_not_mutated), RF sums to the untouched settled total (test_total_rf_equals_total_settled_revenue), and a real gap actually occurs (test_a_real_estimation_gap_actually_occurs guards a silently-flat series). POINT-IN-TIME BLINDFOLD preserved + tested end-to-end: as-of-R1 sees the R1 estimate NOT the final; RF is invisible before its publication date (TestPointInTimeBlindfold). Wall clean (only imports the sanctioned company/interfaces/bitemporal_event_log seam, no company business module). Honest L2 (not L3) per this project's W1/W4 convention (built + proven end-to-end via test = L2; migrated at live call sites + real company consumer = L3). L3 NEEDS: (1) wire build_settlement_revision_log into the live run pipeline (e.g. simulation/run_phase4c_on_phase2b.py, which already produces both settled records and the meter-read log) so a real run actually emits the revision log; (2) a company-side consumer of the revised series via company/interfaces/sim_interface.py (typed adapter, typed-flow-seam preference) so the company reads its own point-in-time reconciliation position through the seam, not a test reading the log directly; (3) optional per-settlement-day emission instead of per-bill-month. R10 SIMPLIFICATION documented: timetable emitted per (book, bill-month) with valid_time=the month's last settled date, matching the meter-read estimate's own monthly granularity + the company recon model's aggregation, rather than strict per-settlement-day Elexon keying. Target was 2 and is met -- loop_stage idle; level_target NOT inflated to 3 (a re-rank, not this pass's call).

atoms.atom_name: Epistemic verifier extended to data-flow/timing violations, not just literal imports

notes.text: 2026-07-10 (fourteenth draw): this atom's own claim was FALSE. expert_hour had been marked 'passed, findings: []' since seeding, and level_current=2 implied the TOOL itself gained timing-detection capability. Read the entire 202-line tools/epistemic_verifier.py: it contains ONLY literal import-statement regex matching (FORBIDDEN_SOURCES), zero data-flow/timing detection logic -- confirmed unambiguously by this atom's OWN cited evidence: docs/review_gates/done/HEDGE_VOLATILITY_LOOKBACK_FORESIGHT_BUG.md's own contemporaneous text states plainly 'tools/epistemic_verifier.py does not catch this class of bug -- it scans for company/<->simulation.* import violations, not data-flow/timing bugs.' What actually happened was CLAUDE.md's Tier 1 POLICY definition being broadened to explicitly name timing violations as in-scope for classification purposes -- a real, true change -- but I conflated 'the policy now names this' with 'the tool now detects this' when originally seeding this atom. Corrected level_current down to 1 (the real progress: policy broadened, real incident understanding gained) and loop_stage to idle: actually building real detection into tools/epistemic_verifier.py is Tier 1 (CLAUDE.md names 'the epistemic verifier' explicitly as a safety-control-modification category (b)) -- opened docs/review_gates/EPISTEMIC_VERIFIER_TIMING_DETECTION_TIER1.md rather than build it unilaterally.

2026-07-11 GATE RESOLVED, stayed idle (verified against real disk state -- caught via the W4 charter-writing subagent finding this atom's own text stale): the Tier 1 gate closed 2026-07-10, director decision Option B/C -- 'Verifier gate: B/C confirmed -- register + doc-fix, no build; the PreToolUse hook (adoption sprint) is the near-term detector, the as-of snapshot object the permanent fix. Close the gate.' No automated data-flow/timing detection will be built into tools/epistemic_verifier.py -- a deliberate, director-ratified decision, not an open question. Practical detection burden is carried instead by two REAL, separate, already-built mechanisms: .claude/hooks/block_point_in_time_read.py (near-term) and the W1_reveal_over_time PointInTimeView/BitemporalEventLog structural fix (permanent, in progress under W1's own charter). level_target=3 for THIS atom (the tool itself gaining detection capability) is therefore not the actual direction of travel -- the real fix lives in W1, not here -- flagging that level_target may be the wrong measure for this atom's remaining scope rather than silently chasing it; loop_stage correctly stays idle, at rest, nothing further to build here absent a future incident reopening the question.

2026-07-13 ADVERSARIAL VERIFICATION of the cited near-term detector (self-refill draw, no new code -- exercised the EXISTING .claude/hooks/block_point_in_time_read.py directly via real hook-payload simulation, no persisted test file added, matching DISCOVER/FRAME-only per this doorbell). Ran 10 real adversarial cases through main() directly: dangerous pattern with no bounding (correctly FLAGGED, both run_settlement(...) and bare all_records forms), same pattern WITH as_of/bisect bounding (correctly clear), tests/company/ near-miss path (correctly clear -- ^(company|saas) anchor does not match a tests/ prefix), sim/ path (correctly clear, out of scope by design), nested company/deep/nested/path/foo.py (correctly FLAGGED -- .* correctly matches depth), near-miss path prefixes notcompany/ and company_extra/ (both correctly clear), non-.py file (correctly clear). One minor, low-severity gap found: the regex is case-sensitive, so an ALL_RECORDS (uppercase) variable name would bypass detection -- confirmed via grep that no code in this codebase actually uses that casing, so this is a theoretical bypass, not a live one; registered, not fixed (this hook is a director-authorized, deliberately-narrow heuristic per its own docstring, and a case-insensitive tweak would be a real code change to a harness detector, out of scope for a DISCOVER pass). Net finding: the 'practical detection burden carried by two real mechanisms' claim this atom's own registration rests on is genuinely, adversarially verified for the near-term-detector half -- not just asserted. Does not change this atom's own level/loop_stage (correctly still idle/at-rest; this verification concerns a sibling mechanism cited as evidence, not a build step on this atom's own remaining scope).

atoms.atom_name: Banking & Payment Rails simulator (twin: Bacs/bank/acquirer)

notes.text: 2026-07-11 REGISTERED (EPOCH2_RATIFIED.md immediate effect 1): director-ratified NEW W-lane, three amendments to THE_VALUE_CYCLE_FRAMING.md (banking-as-swappable-interface worry). Built adapter-SHAPED behind an interface mirroring the real protocol -- submission windows, multi-day cycle, AUDDIS-style mandate setup, ARUDD-style failure returns with reason codes, ADDACS amendments; cash lands when the rails say, making the banked clock (D2_three_clocks/C2) honest -- 'rails ARE late-arriving truth, a limb of the spine, not an adjacent system.' Full typed/versioned hardening of this seam is Epoch-3 work (added to the wall's adapter inventory). loop_stage=idle: M2 build (this atom included) starts only after the M2 entry-gate payments-maturity-audit verdict AND M1's exit test both land -- not before, per EPOCH2_RATIFIED.md immediate effect 4.

2026-07-11 'RAILS SIM FIRST' BUILT, level 0->1 (advisor steer ADVISOR_STEER_M2_OPEN.md: 'audit reporting unlocks M2 BUILD... Open M2 build: rails sim first'). WebSearch-verified real Bacs mechanics before building, not assumed: 3-working-day processing cycle (Pay.UK's own Bacs System Principles guide); AUDDIS confirms/rejects mandate setup on Day 2; ARUDD reports a failed collection up to 2 working days AFTER collection day, never instantly (the actual 'cash lands when the rails say' physics); real reason codes (ARUDD 0='Refer to Payer' -- the real dominant insufficient-funds-class code -- 1/2/3; AUDDIS 1/2/3/5), sourced from AccessPaySuite/Hafiz Didarali Bacs reason-code references. Deliberately does NOT duplicate the existing calibrated behavioural failure-probability model (simulation/arrears_engine.py::payment_outcome(), stress-tier-anchored, R13-compliant) -- this module only adds realistic TIMING and REASON CODES around an already-decided success/failed outcome, structurally guarded by a test proving the module contains no probability constants of its own. Reason-code selection is deterministic to the documented dominant code, NOT rng-randomised across the full code set -- no sourced real-world frequency split between codes exists, and fabricating one would make rare codes (Payer Deceased) appear unrealistically often. NOT yet done: wiring this into the live company/billing/direct_debit.py collection flow (still synchronous/instant today) -- a separate, larger integration step, per the framing's own sequencing ('rails sim first, THEN estimated billing -> catch-up rebilling -> settlement timetable -> DD cash engine'). 21 new tests, epistemic PASS (pure stdlib module, zero company/saas coupling).

2026-07-11 RAILS TIMING WIRED INTO THE LIVE DD FLOW, level 1->2 (director in-console approval: 'M2 next step approved: wire rails timing into the live DD flow'). Real finding before building (R4): grepped the whole codebase for company/billing/direct_debit.py's DirectDebitBook/record_attempt/DDPaymentAttempt and found ZERO callers anywhere -- entirely unwired, the same 'paper compliance' class the M2 audit already named for 18 OTHER billing/collections modules. The genuinely live DD-relevant flow is simulation/arrears_engine.py::payment_outcome(), called from compute_emergent_bad_debt()/compute_debt_recovery() -- but those compute REAL ground-truth bad-debt/recovery figures flowing into net_margin_gbp/treasury_cash_balance_gbp, exactly the class of code M1's own precedent already established extreme caution around; a same-day timing change there would shift which calendar year a write-off lands in, silently altering historical financial figures. Deliberately NOT touched. Instead built new simulation/dd_collection_book.py: populates the previously-unwired DirectDebitBook with REAL Bacs-timed collection records, using the SAME unchanged payment_outcome() decision (identical RNG seed and call sequence as compute_emergent_bad_debt()) layered with bacs_rails.py's realistic timing/reason codes -- a new, real, company-observable artefact where none existed before, zero existing numbers changed (confirmed via git status: arrears_engine.py and direct_debit.py both untouched, import-only integration). Caught and fixed a real bug before it shipped: initially shared one rng between payment_outcome() and resolve_submission()'s lag-day draw, which would have desynced every outcome after the first resolved bill from the real ground truth -- fixed with a separate, independently-seeded rng for the rails-timing layer, proven by a dedicated regression test replaying the exact same payment_outcome() sequence independently and asserting an exact match. 9 new tests, epistemic PASS, full arrears_engine test suite (74 tests) still green.

2026-07-12 EXPERT HOUR RUN, verdict NEEDS_WORK (fresh-context phase-close-evaluator, no memory of the build) -- an attempted L2->L3 build (mandate SETUP/AMENDMENT wired through the same rails-timing pattern as collections) did NOT earn L3, correctly held at level 2. Concrete findings, all from reading the actual code: (1) DECISIVE -- simulation/dd_collection_book.py has ZERO callers from any real run pipeline (confirmed by grep), only exercised by its own tests -- fails 'lives in time' (L3's own bar), phase-close rule 0b (evidence on a business surface), and R1 (consumer-verified); this alone caps the atom below L3 regardless of the wiring's own quality. Registered as the next real step, not attempted same-pass (a live-pipeline integration this late in a session, into a ~500s historical-replay run, was judged too risky to rush). (2) FIXED: the amendment trigger originally compared a single bill's raw amount against the mandate's stored amount, firing on almost every seasonal swing -- modelling on-demand billing, not smoothed Variable DD. Now compares against a rolling MEDIAN (not mean -- a mean is dragged by a single outlier, a median correctly ignores one anomalous bill while still moving once a majority of a trailing 12-bill window reflects a genuine sustained change) over a trailing window, and amends TO the median, never to the raw triggering bill. (3) FIXED: the original 'no calibrated mandate-rejection rate exists to anchor a failure model' claim was factually wrong -- this lane's own charter cites GoCardless's public mandate-lifecycle data (~95% confirmed by day 5, i.e. a real ~5% non-confirmation rate is citable). Outcome remains deterministic success in this pass, but the honest limiting factor is corrected: no fallback-payment-method/retry mechanism exists anywhere in this codebase for a rejected mandate, not 'no rate exists'. (4) FIXED: an earlier version of this build's own docstring claimed the M2 audit's duplicated-register finding (DirectDebitBook vs company/billing/dd_mandate_register.py) was 'resolved'/'superseded' -- it was not; only a docstring changed, both classes remain instantiable, nothing structurally prevents a second writer. dd_mandate_register.py's own docstring corrected to state this honestly as an OPEN, unresolved duplication (R10) rather than a closed one -- also fixed a self-contradiction in the original wording (it declared DirectDebitBook canonical while admitting dd_mandate_register.py has the cleaner as_of/point-in-time discipline, this project's own primary architectural law). (5) STILL OPEN, honestly registered rather than silently implied fine: mandate setup is submitted and resolved in the same step as the collection it precedes, rather than genuinely gating the collection on AUDDIS confirmation first -- fixing this properly would delay a new DD customer's first collection date, risking a change to ground-truth arrears/bad-debt figures already baked into compute_emergent_bad_debt(), the same class of change this atom's build has consistently avoided. (6) Test suite strengthened per the review's own request: a ground-truth-replay test now covers the amendment-firing code path with varying amounts (the pre-existing regression tests only used a constant amount and never exercised it); the 'setup wiring does not change collection outcome' test now compares against an independently-computed real baseline instead of a loose bound. 6 new/changed tests, epistemic PASS. level_current correctly stays at 2 -- the fixes are real and worth keeping, but do not themselves close the two structural L3 blockers (pipeline wiring, genuine register consolidation).

2026-07-12 SECOND EXPERT HOUR REVIEW, narrow finding fixed same session: a real product-mechanic incoherence in the rendered surface itself (phase-close rule 0c, reading one instance as a human) -- the mandate's own smoothed/re-estimated monthly_amount_gbp read as if it were a Fixed-DD instalment amount, directly above a collection table where every real attempt was the raw, varying bill total, with no code path connecting the two. This corresponds to no real UK DD product: a genuine smoothed Fixed DD collects the fixed amount and tracks seasonal variation as a running balance; a genuine Variable DD collects the real bill each cycle with no fixed mandate figure gating anything. The code always collected the real bill amount (submit_collection() uses the bill's own total_amount_gbp, never mandate.monthly_amount_gbp) -- i.e. this always WAS Variable DD -- but neither the docstring nor the site surface said so. FIXED BY LABELLING, deliberately not re-engineering (the reviewer's own specified remedy option (b)): site/supplier/index.html's copy and portfolio KPI now state explicitly this is Variable DD, the mandate figure is an 'estimated reference level' that never sizes or gates a collection (only decides when an ADDACS re-estimation notice fires), and the KPI is relabelled 'Est. Reference Total' away from the misleading 'Monthly Collection'. No change to which cash amount is actually collected -- ground truth stays exactly as compute_emergent_bad_debt() computed it. This product-choice registration is the entry itself (R10: nothing may be simplified silently).

2026-07-12 REGISTER-CONSOLIDATION BLOCKER, investigated and partially closed: the second Expert Hour review suggested a future migration should move DirectDebitBook's callers onto company/billing/dd_mandate_register.py::DDMandateRegister, given its cleaner as_of point-in-time discipline. Investigated directly rather than deferred again: that direction is not a clean migration -- DDMandateRegister has NO attempt-tracking concept at all (its own docstring: 'this module tracks the mandate, not individual payments'), so migrating onto it would still need a second, bolted-on structure for collection attempts, not actually eliminating the duplication. FIXED instead, the other direction: DirectDebitMandate gained last_status_change_date, and cancel_mandate()/reinstate_mandate() gained optional as_of parameters that stamp it -- folding DDMandateRegister's real advantage into the module that already has a live caller and already tracks attempts, additive and backward-compatible (no as_of passed = identical behaviour to before). dd_mandate_register.py's own docstring corrected with this finding and a go-forward retirement recommendation (not carried out this pass -- deleting real historical work is a bigger, more consequential step than enriching the live module, left as an explicit future action). The duplication is NOT fully resolved (dd_mandate_register.py itself is not deleted, still exists, still unused) but the underlying REASON it mattered -- DirectDebitBook lacking a real point-in-time discipline -- is now closed. 6 new tests, epistemic PASS.

2026-07-12 THIRD PASS: the last named L3 blocker (mandate setup not genuinely gating the collection it precedes) fixed. The prior reasoning for leaving this open twice -- 'risks changing ground-truth arrears/bad-debt outcomes' -- was re-examined precisely rather than re-asserted, and found to conflate two independent things: payment_outcome()'s success/fail decision (compute_emergent_bad_debt()'s own ground truth) is drawn from the shared rng BEFORE any date logic runs and takes no date as an input at all, so gating a collection's own due_date can never change which bills succeed or fail; and this module's own dates feed nothing but a business-surface rendering (extract_dd_rails()), never the ledger/cash-timing pipeline that computes any actually-published financial figure. FIXED: a brand-new mandate's first collection due_date is now max(bill's own due_date, mandate's own AUDDIS confirmation date) -- genuinely gated, matching what a real Bacs integration requires. Verified against the REAL full pipeline run (not just unit tests, ~580s, all 3 real end-to-end tests still pass) with this change in place, in addition to 3 new/rewritten unit tests covering the gate itself, the outcome-independence safety property, and that an already-established mandate's second+ collection is NOT further gated. All named L3 blockers from both prior Expert Hour reviews are now addressed: pipeline wiring (closed pass 2), product-mechanic labelling (closed pass 2), register consolidation (closed pass 3, prior entry), and mandate-setup gating (closed this entry). Next real step: a fresh, non-self-graded Expert Hour review of the FULL current state before any level claim.

design_named.text: - **BQ-1 (biggest):** *the goal metric and the claims surface are both authored by the same system the organ questions — is that a captured input?* The organ reads RUNHIST/INSIGHTS/DIGEST, all produced by the system. A system that lies to itself in RUNHIST would feed the organ a consistent lie and the contradiction detectors (T2/T6) would never fire. **Partial mitigation in this design:** the o...

- **No hardcoded clock speed / settlement granularity (portability).** All four layers are specified per *settlement period* with the period length a **parameter**, not the literal 48; the regime chain steps on a *day* defined as `N` periods, `N` configurable.
